mainEntity.name: Why does my child is constantly crashing into things, jumping off furniture, and squeezing people too hard when they hug. they can't sit still for even five minutes — they're always fidgeting, rocking, or finding ways to move. at school, they tip their chair back, wrap their legs around the chair legs, and chew on everything. teachers say they're disruptive, but i can see it's not defiance — it's like their body is searching for something it can't find. when they're really dysregulated, they might hit or push, and i don't think they even realize how hard they're being. they seem to have no sense of their own strength. but here's the strange thing: after certain activities — swimming, carrying groceries, playing rough with their dad, jumping on a trampoline — they're calmer, more focused, almost like a different child. the effect doesn't last long, but it's real. the occupational therapist mentioned 'proprioceptive input' and 'heavy work activities' but i don't fully understand what that means. how is pushing something heavy supposed to help with attention? why does my child need this when other kids don't? and how do i provide it throughout the day without it taking over our lives? i want to understand what their body is telling us and how to give it what it needs?

acceptedAnswer.text: PROPRIOCEPTIVE PROCESSING DIFFERENCES / SENSORY MODULATION CHALLENGES / SENSORY SEEKING BEHAVIOR PATTERN / UNDER-RESPONSIVE PROPRIOCEPTIVE SYSTEM! Proprioception is the sensory system that tells us where our body is in space, how much force we're using, and how our limbs are positioned — all without looking. Receptors in muscles, joints, and tendons send this information to the brain constantly. When the proprioceptive system is under-responsive or processes input inefficiently, children may not get enough information from everyday movements. Their nervous system then seeks additional input through crashing, jumping, pushing, squeezing, and constant movement — behaviors that look disruptive but are actually attempts at self-regulation. 'Heavy work' refers to activities that provide intense proprioceptive input through pushing, pulling, carrying, lifting, and resistance. This input has an organizing and calming effect on the nervous system. When provided strategically throughout the day (a 'sensory diet'), heavy work can significantly improve attention, behavior, and self-regulation.

mainEntity.name: What is Proprioceptive Processing Differences / Sensory Modulation Challenges / Sensory Seeking Behavior Pattern / Under-Responsive Proprioceptive System?

acceptedAnswer.text: Proprioception is the sensory system that tells us where our body is in space, how much force we're using, and how our limbs are positioned — all without looking. Receptors in muscles, joints, and tendons send this information to the brain constantly. When the proprioceptive system is under-responsive or processes input inefficiently, children may not get enough information from everyday movements. Their nervous system then seeks additional input through crashing, jumping, pushing, squeezing, and constant movement — behaviors that look disruptive but are actually attempts at self-regulation. 'Heavy work' refers to activities that provide intense proprioceptive input through pushing, pulling, carrying, lifting, and resistance. This input has an organizing and calming effect on the nervous system. When provided strategically throughout the day (a 'sensory diet'), heavy work can significantly improve attention, behavior, and self-regulation. Common signs include: Constantly moving, fidgeting, or seeking physical input, Crashes into people, furniture, or walls on purpose, Jumps off furniture, beds, or any available height, Squeezes people too hard during hugs, Doesn't seem to know their own strength.

mainEntity.name: How does a Weighted Blanket help with proprioceptive processing differences?

acceptedAnswer.text: Weighted blankets provide sustained, even deep pressure across the body, activating proprioceptors throughout. This input has a calming, organizing effect on the nervous system — similar to a firm hug or being swaddled. The weight should be approximately 10% of the child's body weight. Weighted blankets are particularly effective for calming before sleep, during transitions, during seated activities requiring focus, and when the child is becoming dysregulated. The pressure helps the nervous system shift from an alert, seeking state to a calm, organized state. Unlike active heavy work, weighted blankets provide passive input — the child simply lies under or wraps in the blanket. This makes them ideal for rest periods, reading time, or any situation where active movement isn't possible. Many children who struggle with sleep onset find weighted blankets particularly helpful. Price: ₹2,500–6,000. DIY option: Making a weighted blanket: 1) Choose a soft, durable outer fabric (cotton, minky fleece, or sensory-appropriate material), 2) Create a grid pattern of pockets (typically 4-6 inch squares) to prevent weight from shifting, 3) Fill pockets evenly with weighted material — poly pellets (plastic beads) are most common; glass beads are heavier for same volume; rice or dried beans work but can mold if wet, 4) Weight calculation: child's body weight × 10% (e.g., 30 kg child = 3 kg blanket). Add 0.5-1 kg for preference, but never exceed 10% + 2 kg, 5) Ensure even weight distribution across all pockets, 6) Double-stitch all seams for durability and safety. Note: Commercial blankets are often worth the investment for safety and durability. Always supervise young children with weighted blankets. Safety: Never use weighted blankets on children under 2 years or children who cannot remove the blanket independently. Maximum 10% of body weight plus 1-2 kg. Supervise initial uses. Do not use during active distress or for restraint.

mainEntity.name: How does a Therapy/Exercise Ball help with proprioceptive processing differences?

acceptedAnswer.text: Large therapy balls (also called exercise or stability balls) are versatile tools for heavy work. When a child lies over the ball for 'squishes,' the adult applies firm pressure through the ball into the child's body, providing intense proprioceptive input. The child can also bounce on the ball (rhythmic vestibular-proprioceptive input), roll over it (deep pressure plus movement), or push against it (resistance). Using a ball as alternative seating engages core muscles continuously, providing constant low-level proprioceptive input during seated activities. The unstable surface requires constant micro-adjustments, keeping the proprioceptive system engaged. For sensory-seeking children, a therapy ball transforms passive sitting into active, organizing input. Many children who struggle to sit still in chairs can focus much better on therapy balls because the ball provides the input their nervous systems crave. Price: ₹800–2,000. DIY option: Selecting and using a therapy ball: 1) Size selection: When child sits on ball, hips and knees should be at 90 degrees. Typically: 45cm for children under 4'10", 55cm for 4'10"-5'5", 65cm for taller. 2) Inflation: Slightly underinflated is more stable and forgiving. 3) 'Squish' technique: Child lies face down over ball, arms and legs relaxed. Adult places hands on child's back and provides firm, rhythmic pressure downward through the ball. 10-20 squishes typically. Always check child's comfort. 4) Ball seating: Consider a ball chair with base for classroom use (prevents rolling away). Ensure desk height is appropriate. Start with short periods (5-10 minutes) and increase. 5) Bouncing: Child sits on ball and bounces. Can add counting or songs for rhythm. Very organizing input. Store balls safely when not in use — they roll! Safety: Supervise ball activities, especially bouncing. Ensure balls are appropriate size and inflation. Use on clear, non-slip surfaces. Check for wear and replace if damaged.

mainEntity.name: How does a Resistance Bands/Therabands help with proprioceptive processing differences?

acceptedAnswer.text: Resistance bands provide portable, adjustable heavy work through pulling and stretching. The resistance engages muscles throughout their range of motion, sending proprioceptive signals continuously. Unlike weights, bands can be used in any direction and are silent, making them ideal for classroom or quiet settings. Bands can be tied around chair legs for leg pressing while seated, wrapped around the child for arm exercises, used for tug-of-war games, or incorporated into movement breaks. The resistance level is easily adjusted by using different band strengths (colors indicate resistance levels) or by changing grip position. For sensory-seeking children who need to move during seated activities, a resistance band on their chair provides an acceptable, quiet outlet. They can press against the band with their feet or legs while still appearing to sit appropriately. Price: ₹300–800. DIY option: Implementing resistance bands: 1) Band selection: Start with lighter resistance and increase as needed. Common progression: yellow (light) → red (medium) → green (heavy) → blue (very heavy), though colors vary by brand. 2) Chair bands: Loop band around front two chair legs. Child can press feet against band while seated. Replace when stretched out. 3) Arm exercises: Child holds band ends and stretches apart, pulls overhead, or performs rowing motions. 4) Tug-of-war: Two children or adult-child pull against each other through the band. 5) Creative uses: Wall push-offs with band around back, monster walks with band around ankles, marching with band resistance. 6) Durability: Check bands regularly for wear, especially near attachment points. Store away from heat and sun. Consider fabric-covered bands for children who might bite or pick at rubber. Safety: Check bands for wear before each use — snapping bands can hurt. Supervise use to ensure appropriate stretching. Never pull toward face. Replace worn bands.

mainEntity.name: How does a Weighted Vest or Compression Vest help with proprioceptive processing differences?

acceptedAnswer.text: Weighted vests distribute weight across the shoulders and torso, providing proprioceptive input throughout wear. The added weight makes every movement heavier, engaging muscles more intensely and increasing proprioceptive feedback. Compression vests (without weight) provide sustained deep pressure around the torso, similar to a constant hug. Some vests combine both weight and compression. Vests are particularly useful during activities requiring focus and calm — classroom work, homework, therapy sessions, transitions. The input is constant and doesn't require any action from the child, making it 'background' regulation. Research suggests wearing periods of 20-30 minutes followed by breaks are most effective; continuous wear may lead to habituation. Weight should not exceed 5-10% of the child's body weight. Choose vests that look like regular clothing for social acceptance. Price: ₹2,000–5,000. DIY option: Using vests effectively: 1) Weight calculation: Maximum 5-10% of body weight. Start lighter and adjust based on response. More is not always better. 2) Wearing schedule: 20-30 minutes on, 20-30 minutes off. Continuous wear reduces effectiveness. Time vest use for most challenging periods (demanding schoolwork, transitions, overwhelming environments). 3) Compression alternatives: Snug-fitting rash guards, compression athletic wear, layered tight clothing can provide similar (though less intense) input without specialized equipment. 4) Social considerations: Choose vests that look like regular clothing (fleece vests with hidden weights, athletic-style compression wear). Avoid medical-looking equipment for social inclusion. 5) Monitoring: Watch for red marks, discomfort, or overheating. Child should be able to request removal. Never use for restraint. 6) DIY weighted vest: Small weights can be sewn into pockets of a vest, but commercial products are typically safer and more evenly distributed. Safety: Maximum 5-10% body weight. Never use as restraint. Child should be able to remove independently. Watch for overheating. Follow wearing schedule to prevent habituation.

mainEntity.name: How does a Body Sock / Lycra Resistance Tunnel help with proprioceptive processing differences?

acceptedAnswer.text: A body sock is a stretchy, enclosed fabric sack that the child climbs into. As they move inside, the fabric provides resistance in every direction, creating intense proprioceptive input throughout the body. The enclosure also provides deep pressure and, for some children, a sense of security and defined body boundaries. Body socks are highly engaging — children naturally want to move, stretch, roll, and play inside them, making heavy work feel like play. The fabric can be pushed, stretched, and shaped, providing resistance to every movement. For children who struggle with body awareness (not knowing where their body ends and space begins), the body sock provides constant feedback about body position and boundaries. The activity is naturally self-limiting — children typically use body socks in bursts of high-intensity input, then emerge when regulated. Price: ₹1,500–3,500. DIY option: Using body socks effectively: 1) Size selection: The sock should be snug when the child is curled up but stretchy enough to allow full extension of arms and legs. Too loose = insufficient input. Too tight = frustrating and restrictive. 2) Introduction: Some children love body socks immediately; others need gradual introduction. Start with just putting arms in, then add legs, then full body. Never force. 3) Activities inside: Yoga poses, rolling, making shapes, freeze dance, obstacle courses, crawling races. The movement is the input. 4) Combining with other input: Add music with strong beat, roll over therapy ball while in sock, play freeze games. 5) DIY alternative: A stretchy pillowcase can work for smaller children or initial introduction. Large Lycra fabric sewn into a tube with one open end. Commercial socks are more durable and appropriately sized. 6) Supervision: Children should be able to exit independently. Face opening must always be clear. Supervise all use. Safety: Child must be able to exit independently at any time. Face opening must remain accessible. Supervise all use. Not for children who panic in enclosed spaces. Check for wear and replace when fabric loses stretch.

mainEntity.name: How does a Mini Trampoline / Rebounder help with proprioceptive processing differences?

acceptedAnswer.text: Jumping on a trampoline provides intense proprioceptive input through the joints with each landing, combined with vestibular input from the up-and-down movement. The combination is highly regulating for most children. The impact of landing sends strong signals through leg and spine joints, while the bouncing motion provides rhythmic, predictable input that organizes the nervous system. For sensory-seeking children, a trampoline provides a safe, appropriate outlet for the jumping they're already driven to do. A mini trampoline (rebounder) is small enough for indoor use and can be part of a daily sensory diet. Many families place a mini trampoline in a corner of the living room or child's bedroom for on-demand use. Even a few minutes of jumping can significantly shift a child's regulation state — calming hyperactivity, increasing alertness when sluggish, or preparing for focused activities. Price: ₹2,000–5,000. DIY option: Implementing trampoline heavy work: 1) Trampoline selection: Mini trampolines (rebounders) with handlebar are safest for indoor use. Ensure weight rating exceeds child's weight. Look for quiet springs or bungee cord models for indoor use. 2) Placement: Clear area with nothing to bump into during bouncing. Away from furniture, walls with hard corners, windows. Soft flooring underneath is ideal. 3) Structured activities: Counting jumps, jumping to music, freeze bouncing, calling out movements ('jump high!' 'tiny jumps!' 'stop!'). 4) Integration into routine: Morning jumping before school, after-school decompression, transition tool between activities, pre-homework alerting. 5) Duration: Most children self-regulate duration. Typical effective dose is 5-15 minutes. Watch for signs of overstimulation (getting wilder rather than calmer). 6) Supervision: Required for young children or those with balance challenges. Handlebar models add safety. Safety: Clear surrounding area completely. Use handlebar models for safety. Check trampoline regularly for wear. One child at a time. Supervise young children. Stop if child becomes more dysregulated rather than calmer.

@graph.@id: https://pinnacleblooms.org/intervention-techniques/sensory processing / occupational therapy / self-regulation / proprioceptive processing / behavioral support/proprioceptive input / sensory modulation / arousal regulation / attention support / calming strategies / body awareness/9-materials-that-help-with-heavy-work-activities#howto
@graph.name: How to Help a Child with Proprioceptive Processing Differences
@graph.description: A graded approach using evidence-based materials. Proprioception is the 'hidden' sensory system that tells us where our body is in space, how much force we're using, and the position of our limbs — all without looking. Proprioceptive receptors in muscles, joints, and tendons send continuous information to the brain. When this system is under-responsive or processes input inefficiently, the nervous system doesn't receive enough information from everyday movements. The child then seeks additional input through crashing, jumping, pushing, squeezing, and constant movement — behaviors that look disruptive but are actually regulatory attempts. Heavy work activities provide intense proprioceptive input that satisfies this seeking and has a calming, organizing effect. The input can be alerting (for under-aroused children) or calming (for over-aroused children) — proprioception is uniquely regulating in both directions. A 'sensory diet' that includes regular heavy work throughout the day can significantly improve attention, behavior, and self-regulation.

'Heavy work' refers to activities that provide intense proprioceptive input through pushing, pulling, carrying, lifting, and resistance. This input has an organizing and calming effect on the nervous system. When provided strategically throughout the day (a 'sensory diet'), heavy work can significantly improve attention, behavior, and self-regulation. @graph.name: 9 Materials for Proprioceptive Processing Differences

item.name: Weighted Blanket
item.description: Weighted blankets provide sustained, even deep pressure across the body, activating proprioceptors throughout. This input has a calming, organizing effect on the nervous system — similar to a firm hug or being swaddled. The weight should be approximately 10% of the child's body weight. Weighted blankets are particularly effective for calming before sleep, during transitions, during seated activities requiring focus, and when the child is becoming dysregulated. The pressure helps the nervous system shift from an alert, seeking state to a calm, organized state. Unlike active heavy work, weighted blankets provide passive input — the child simply lies under or wraps in the blanket. This makes them ideal for rest periods, reading time, or any situation where active movement isn't possible. Many children who struggle with sleep onset find weighted blankets particularly helpful.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Making a weighted blanket: 1) Choose a soft, durable outer fabric (cotton, minky fleece, or sensory-appropriate material), 2) Create a grid pattern of pockets (typically 4-6 inch squares) to prevent weight from shifting, 3) Fill pockets evenly with weighted material — poly pellets (plastic beads) are most common; glass beads are heavier for same volume; rice or dried beans work but can mold if wet, 4) Weight calculation: child's body weight × 10% (e.g., 30 kg child = 3 kg blanket). Add 0.5-1 kg for preference, but never exceed 10% + 2 kg, 5) Ensure even weight distribution across all pockets, 6) Double-stitch all seams for durability and safety. Note: Commercial blankets are often worth the investment for safety and durability. Always supervise young children with weighted blankets.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Never use weighted blankets on children under 2 years or children who cannot remove the blanket independently. Maximum 10% of body weight plus 1-2 kg. Supervise initial uses. Do not use during active distress or for restraint.

item.name: Therapy/Exercise Ball
item.description: Large therapy balls (also called exercise or stability balls) are versatile tools for heavy work. When a child lies over the ball for 'squishes,' the adult applies firm pressure through the ball into the child's body, providing intense proprioceptive input. The child can also bounce on the ball (rhythmic vestibular-proprioceptive input), roll over it (deep pressure plus movement), or push against it (resistance). Using a ball as alternative seating engages core muscles continuously, providing constant low-level proprioceptive input during seated activities. The unstable surface requires constant micro-adjustments, keeping the proprioceptive system engaged. For sensory-seeking children, a therapy ball transforms passive sitting into active, organizing input. Many children who struggle to sit still in chairs can focus much better on therapy balls because the ball provides the input their nervous systems crave.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Selecting and using a therapy ball: 1) Size selection: When child sits on ball, hips and knees should be at 90 degrees. Typically: 45cm for children under 4'10", 55cm for 4'10"-5'5", 65cm for taller. 2) Inflation: Slightly underinflated is more stable and forgiving. 3) 'Squish' technique: Child lies face down over ball, arms and legs relaxed. Adult places hands on child's back and provides firm, rhythmic pressure downward through the ball. 10-20 squishes typically. Always check child's comfort. 4) Ball seating: Consider a ball chair with base for classroom use (prevents rolling away). Ensure desk height is appropriate. Start with short periods (5-10 minutes) and increase. 5) Bouncing: Child sits on ball and bounces. Can add counting or songs for rhythm. Very organizing input. Store balls safely when not in use — they roll!

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Supervise ball activities, especially bouncing. Ensure balls are appropriate size and inflation. Use on clear, non-slip surfaces. Check for wear and replace if damaged.

item.name: Resistance Bands/Therabands
item.description: Resistance bands provide portable, adjustable heavy work through pulling and stretching. The resistance engages muscles throughout their range of motion, sending proprioceptive signals continuously. Unlike weights, bands can be used in any direction and are silent, making them ideal for classroom or quiet settings. Bands can be tied around chair legs for leg pressing while seated, wrapped around the child for arm exercises, used for tug-of-war games, or incorporated into movement breaks. The resistance level is easily adjusted by using different band strengths (colors indicate resistance levels) or by changing grip position. For sensory-seeking children who need to move during seated activities, a resistance band on their chair provides an acceptable, quiet outlet. They can press against the band with their feet or legs while still appearing to sit appropriately.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Implementing resistance bands: 1) Band selection: Start with lighter resistance and increase as needed. Common progression: yellow (light) → red (medium) → green (heavy) → blue (very heavy), though colors vary by brand. 2) Chair bands: Loop band around front two chair legs. Child can press feet against band while seated. Replace when stretched out. 3) Arm exercises: Child holds band ends and stretches apart, pulls overhead, or performs rowing motions. 4) Tug-of-war: Two children or adult-child pull against each other through the band. 5) Creative uses: Wall push-offs with band around back, monster walks with band around ankles, marching with band resistance. 6) Durability: Check bands regularly for wear, especially near attachment points. Store away from heat and sun. Consider fabric-covered bands for children who might bite or pick at rubber.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Check bands for wear before each use — snapping bands can hurt. Supervise use to ensure appropriate stretching. Never pull toward face. Replace worn bands.

item.name: Weighted Vest or Compression Vest
item.description: Weighted vests distribute weight across the shoulders and torso, providing proprioceptive input throughout wear. The added weight makes every movement heavier, engaging muscles more intensely and increasing proprioceptive feedback. Compression vests (without weight) provide sustained deep pressure around the torso, similar to a constant hug. Some vests combine both weight and compression. Vests are particularly useful during activities requiring focus and calm — classroom work, homework, therapy sessions, transitions. The input is constant and doesn't require any action from the child, making it 'background' regulation. Research suggests wearing periods of 20-30 minutes followed by breaks are most effective; continuous wear may lead to habituation. Weight should not exceed 5-10% of the child's body weight. Choose vests that look like regular clothing for social acceptance.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Using vests effectively: 1) Weight calculation: Maximum 5-10% of body weight. Start lighter and adjust based on response. More is not always better. 2) Wearing schedule: 20-30 minutes on, 20-30 minutes off. Continuous wear reduces effectiveness. Time vest use for most challenging periods (demanding schoolwork, transitions, overwhelming environments). 3) Compression alternatives: Snug-fitting rash guards, compression athletic wear, layered tight clothing can provide similar (though less intense) input without specialized equipment. 4) Social considerations: Choose vests that look like regular clothing (fleece vests with hidden weights, athletic-style compression wear). Avoid medical-looking equipment for social inclusion. 5) Monitoring: Watch for red marks, discomfort, or overheating. Child should be able to request removal. Never use for restraint. 6) DIY weighted vest: Small weights can be sewn into pockets of a vest, but commercial products are typically safer and more evenly distributed.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Maximum 5-10% body weight. Never use as restraint. Child should be able to remove independently. Watch for overheating. Follow wearing schedule to prevent habituation.

item.name: Body Sock / Lycra Resistance Tunnel
item.description: A body sock is a stretchy, enclosed fabric sack that the child climbs into. As they move inside, the fabric provides resistance in every direction, creating intense proprioceptive input throughout the body. The enclosure also provides deep pressure and, for some children, a sense of security and defined body boundaries. Body socks are highly engaging — children naturally want to move, stretch, roll, and play inside them, making heavy work feel like play. The fabric can be pushed, stretched, and shaped, providing resistance to every movement. For children who struggle with body awareness (not knowing where their body ends and space begins), the body sock provides constant feedback about body position and boundaries. The activity is naturally self-limiting — children typically use body socks in bursts of high-intensity input, then emerge when regulated.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Using body socks effectively: 1) Size selection: The sock should be snug when the child is curled up but stretchy enough to allow full extension of arms and legs. Too loose = insufficient input. Too tight = frustrating and restrictive. 2) Introduction: Some children love body socks immediately; others need gradual introduction. Start with just putting arms in, then add legs, then full body. Never force. 3) Activities inside: Yoga poses, rolling, making shapes, freeze dance, obstacle courses, crawling races. The movement is the input. 4) Combining with other input: Add music with strong beat, roll over therapy ball while in sock, play freeze games. 5) DIY alternative: A stretchy pillowcase can work for smaller children or initial introduction. Large Lycra fabric sewn into a tube with one open end. Commercial socks are more durable and appropriately sized. 6) Supervision: Children should be able to exit independently. Face opening must always be clear. Supervise all use.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Child must be able to exit independently at any time. Face opening must remain accessible. Supervise all use. Not for children who panic in enclosed spaces. Check for wear and replace when fabric loses stretch.

item.name: Mini Trampoline / Rebounder
item.description: Jumping on a trampoline provides intense proprioceptive input through the joints with each landing, combined with vestibular input from the up-and-down movement. The combination is highly regulating for most children. The impact of landing sends strong signals through leg and spine joints, while the bouncing motion provides rhythmic, predictable input that organizes the nervous system. For sensory-seeking children, a trampoline provides a safe, appropriate outlet for the jumping they're already driven to do. A mini trampoline (rebounder) is small enough for indoor use and can be part of a daily sensory diet. Many families place a mini trampoline in a corner of the living room or child's bedroom for on-demand use. Even a few minutes of jumping can significantly shift a child's regulation state — calming hyperactivity, increasing alertness when sluggish, or preparing for focused activities.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Implementing trampoline heavy work: 1) Trampoline selection: Mini trampolines (rebounders) with handlebar are safest for indoor use. Ensure weight rating exceeds child's weight. Look for quiet springs or bungee cord models for indoor use. 2) Placement: Clear area with nothing to bump into during bouncing. Away from furniture, walls with hard corners, windows. Soft flooring underneath is ideal. 3) Structured activities: Counting jumps, jumping to music, freeze bouncing, calling out movements ('jump high!' 'tiny jumps!' 'stop!'). 4) Integration into routine: Morning jumping before school, after-school decompression, transition tool between activities, pre-homework alerting. 5) Duration: Most children self-regulate duration. Typical effective dose is 5-15 minutes. Watch for signs of overstimulation (getting wilder rather than calmer). 6) Supervision: Required for young children or those with balance challenges. Handlebar models add safety.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Clear surrounding area completely. Use handlebar models for safety. Check trampoline regularly for wear. One child at a time. Supervise young children. Stop if child becomes more dysregulated rather than calmer.

item.name: Push-Pull Wagon or Weighted Cart
item.description: Pushing and pulling heavy loads is classic heavy work — the muscles work against resistance, sending strong proprioceptive signals throughout the body. A wagon or cart loaded with weight (books, sand bags, water bottles, siblings) requires sustained effort to move, providing continuous input. The activity is also functional and can be incorporated into daily tasks: carrying laundry, moving groceries, helping with household chores. For children who crash and bump seeking input, redirecting that energy into purposeful pushing and pulling provides the same input through an appropriate activity. The child feels helpful rather than disruptive, building self-esteem while meeting sensory needs. Outdoor wagons can be used for neighborhood walks, making heavy work part of family exercise routines.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Implementing push-pull heavy work: 1) Equipment options: Child-sized wheelbarrow, wagon, laundry basket, moving dolly, shopping cart, sled, large cardboard box. Even a heavy backpack while walking. 2) Weight loading: Add weight gradually. Books, water bottles, sand bags, grocery items, or (with caution and consent) a younger sibling. Weight should require effort but be manageable. 3) Functional integration: Carrying laundry to/from machines, moving groceries from car, taking recycling out, helping move items during cleaning, pulling wagon of toys to put away. 4) Distances: Vary based on child's endurance. Start with short distances and increase. Uphill pushing provides more resistance. 5) Games: Delivery driver (deliver items to different rooms), recycling route, construction site helper, moving day. Purpose adds engagement. 6) Outdoor options: Pull wagons on walks, push wheelbarrow in garden, rake and haul leaves/debris.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure load is secure and won't tip. Clear pathways of obstacles. Appropriate weight for child's strength. Supervise especially on inclines. Check equipment for stability.

item.name: Therapy Putty / Resistive Dough
item.description: Therapy putty and resistive play dough provide heavy work for the hands and arms through squeezing, pulling, stretching, and molding. The resistance engages hand muscles intensely, sending proprioceptive input up through the arms. This is particularly useful for children who chew, bite, or fidget with their hands — it provides an appropriate outlet for hand-based sensory seeking. Therapy putty comes in different resistance levels (identified by color), allowing progressive challenge. Activities can be embedded into play (hiding objects in putty to find and retrieve), fine motor practice (making shapes, rolling snakes), or simply squeezing for regulation. For older children, putty in a pocket provides a discrete fidget tool — something to squeeze during class or stressful situations without drawing attention.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Using therapy putty effectively: 1) Resistance progression: Start with softer putty and increase resistance as hand strength builds. Softest (tan/yellow) → medium (red/green) → firm (blue) → extra firm (gray/black). 2) Activities: Roll into balls, stretch into long snakes, hide small objects to find and retrieve, press flat and use cookie cutters, pull apart into pieces, squeeze repeatedly. 3) DIY resistive dough: Mix 2 cups flour, 1 cup salt, 2 tablespoons cream of tartar, 2 tablespoons oil, 2 cups water. Cook over medium heat until it forms a ball. Knead when cool. Add more flour for more resistance. Not as stretchy as commercial putty but provides resistance. 4) Portable use: Small container of putty in pocket or backpack. Discrete squeezing during challenging situations. 5) Maintenance: Keep putty in sealed container. Replace when it becomes dried out or loses resistance. Keep away from carpets and fabric.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Not for children who mouth/eat non-food items. Keep away from hair, carpet, and fabric. Check for latex allergies. Small hidden objects can be choking hazards for young children.

item.name: Crash Pad / Foam Pit
item.description: Children who crash, jump, and throw themselves onto things are seeking intense proprioceptive and vestibular input through impact. A crash pad provides a safe outlet for this drive. Commercial crash pads are large foam-filled cushions designed for jumping and crashing. When the child lands, the foam provides resistance and deep pressure, sending strong proprioceptive signals throughout the body. The crash pad transforms dangerous crashing behavior (onto furniture, into walls, from heights) into safe, appropriate heavy work. The child can jump, dive, belly flop, or roll onto the crash pad as often as they need. Many families find that providing an appropriate crashing outlet reduces dangerous crashing elsewhere — the nervous system's need is being met safely.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Creating and using crash spaces: 1) Commercial crash pads: Large foam-filled cushions, often 4+ feet across. Dense foam provides resistance; shredded foam provides more 'sinking' sensation. Cover should be removable and washable. 2) DIY crash pit: Large duffle bag or mattress cover filled with foam scraps, pillows, or cushions. Bean bag chairs can work for smaller children. Old mattress on floor as crash zone. 3) Pillow mountain: Pile of couch cushions, pillows, and soft items. Requires re-building but costs nothing. 4) Placement: Clear area with nothing hard or sharp nearby. Away from furniture corners, walls, windows. 5) Usage: Jumping from safe height (step stool initially), running and diving, belly flops, rolling, wrestling. 6) Rules: Clear boundaries about safe jumping heights and distances. Crash pad use only — no crashing elsewhere. Check area is clear before jumping.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Clear area of all hard objects. Set safe jumping height limits. One child at a time. No diving headfirst. Check pad landing zone before each jump. Supervise all use initially.

socialMedia.caption: When their body needs more input to feel organized. Some children's nervous systems need extra proprioceptive input — and that's what heavy work provides. Here are 9 materials that help: weighted blankets, therapy balls, resistance bands, compression vests, body socks, trampolines, push-pull wagons, therapy putty, and crash pads. When we meet the need, the seeking often decreases. 💜 #HeavyWork #SensoryProcessing #PinnacleBlooms
